title: Add mmap (memory-mapped files) visualization — file-to-memory mapping with page fault loading
description: Research agents identified mmap as P2 professional need but no task was generated. mmap is central to: database engines (RocksDB, LMDB, SQLite WAL), ML model loading, and shared memory IPC. Visualize: file on disk, process virtual address space, mmap creates direct mapping, page faults on first access load from disk. Source: OSTEP Ch.23, curriculum agent P2.
acceptanceCriteria: mmap concept in Memory Management sub-section
Visual: file → virtual address space → physical memory mapping
Page fault on first access triggers disk read
Comparison: mmap vs buffered read() performance characteristics
Real-world: 'SQLite uses mmap for database file access; Redis uses mmap for RDB snapshots'

title: Add demand paging vs prepaging comparison visualization
description: Research agents identified this as P2 curriculum gap. Demand paging loads pages only on access (page fault). Prepaging loads anticipated pages ahead of time. Visualize: same access pattern, demand paging has faults on every first access, prepaging pre-loads clusters with fewer faults but wasted loads. Could extend existing simulateVirtualMemory with a prepaging mode. Source: Silberschatz Ch.10, OSTEP Ch.21.
acceptanceCriteria: Demand paging mode (current behavior) labeled explicitly
Prepaging mode: loads N adjacent pages on each fault
Side-by-side comparison showing fault count difference
Configurable prepaging window (how many pages to pre-load)
Explains tradeoff: fewer faults but potentially wasted memory

title: Add Unix signals visualization — SIGTERM, SIGKILL, SIGINT, signal handling
description: Unix signals were NEVER identified by any research agent or audit — a genuine blind spot. Signals are: taught in every systems programming course, asked in interviews ('difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL?'), used daily by every engineer (Ctrl+C = SIGINT). Visualize: process receiving signals, signal handler execution, default actions (terminate, stop, ignore), signal masking. Source: OSTEP Ch.5 (process API), man 7 signal.
acceptanceCriteria: Signals concept as sub-section or Learn tab content
Visual: process → signal received → handler dispatched → action taken
Common signals listed: SIGTERM (graceful shutdown), SIGKILL (force kill), SIGINT (Ctrl+C), SIGSEGV (segfault), SIGCHLD (child terminated)
Default vs custom handler comparison
Real-world: 'Kubernetes sends SIGTERM then SIGKILL after grace period'

title: Add RAID visualization — levels 0, 1, 5, 6, 10 with striping/mirroring animation
description: RAID covered in OSTEP Ch.38, curriculum agent rated P3. OSTEP has raid.py simulator. No competitor has a good RAID visualizer. Visualize: disk array with data blocks, show how RAID 0 stripes, RAID 1 mirrors, RAID 5 distributes parity. Disk failure simulation showing data recovery. Source: OSTEP Ch.38.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 RAID levels: 0 (stripe), 1 (mirror), 5 (distributed parity), 6 (double parity), 10 (stripe+mirror)
Visual: N disks with colored data blocks showing placement strategy
Disk failure mode: click a disk to 'fail' it, show data recovery or loss
Performance comparison: read/write throughput per level
Capacity calculation: usable vs total space per level

title: Fix SVG visualizations to scale with browser zoom using viewBox instead of fixed dimensions
description: QA finding: RAG SVG (line 993) and ThreadSync SVG (line 1175) use computed fixed width/height pixel values. At 200% browser zoom, SVGs stay the same size while text doubles — WCAG 1.4.4 failure. Fix: add viewBox attribute, remove fixed width/height, wrap in overflow-x-auto container. Identified in screen reader audit but never became a numbered task.
acceptanceCriteria: Both SVGs use viewBox instead of fixed width/height
SVGs scale proportionally with browser zoom
Wrapped in overflow-x-auto container for horizontal scroll on small viewports
At 200% zoom: SVG content readable without horizontal scroll clipping
No visual regression at default zoom

title: Add maxLength to process name input and handle text overflow in all label displays
description: QA finding: process name input at OSModule.tsx:2422 has no maxLength. A user typing 'My Very Long Process Name That Overflows Everything' breaks process card layout, Gantt chart labels, properties table, and event log. Add maxLength=10 to input, add text truncation with title tooltip to all places names are displayed.
acceptanceCriteria: Process name input has maxLength=10
Resource name input has maxLength=8
All name displays use truncate class with title={fullName} tooltip
Gantt chart labels truncate gracefully at narrow widths
Event log descriptions use full name (no truncation in text)

title: Extract MAX_TICKS constant and remove dead translateAddress export
description: QA findings: (1) MAX_TICKS=200 defined 3 times in thread-sync.ts (lines 65, 183, 322). Extract to a single constant. (2) translateAddress is exported from index.ts:46 but never imported by any consumer — used only internally by simulateVirtualMemory. Remove from index.ts exports or mark as internal.
acceptanceCriteria: Single MAX_TICKS constant at top of thread-sync.ts, used by all 3 simulations
translateAddress removed from index.ts exports (kept as internal function in memory.ts)
No functional change to any simulation

title: Add undo/redo for OS module simulation inputs
description: The canvas store has full undo/redo via UndoManager (100 max entries). The OS module has zero undo capability. If a user accidentally deletes a process, clears the reference string, or modifies a matrix value, the only recovery is page refresh. Add lightweight undo/redo for simulation inputs using the existing zundo middleware pattern.
acceptanceCriteria: Ctrl+Z undoes the last input change (process add/remove, value change, request add/remove)
Ctrl+Shift+Z redoes
Undo/redo buttons visible in toolbar
Works for: process list, reference string, deadlock matrices, alloc requests, sync parameters
Maximum 20 undo states to limit memory
